description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: How do grain size and interfacial properties influence via extrusion in 3D integration TSVs, and what are the implications of these factors on the reliability and thermal cycling behavior of the TSVs?

gold_context_sources.evidence: Via extrusion has been a major concern for the yield and reliability of 3D integration. It manifests the non-recoverable plastic deformation near the top of the via during thermal cycles. ... The average via extrusion was 117 nm for SG TSVs and 147 nm for LG TSVs, indicating a correlation between the average grain size and the amount of via extrusion. ... The plastic extrusion does not vanish after cooling, resulting in a non-zero residual extrusion after a full thermal cycle: ... Increasing the yield strength of Cu would increase the yield temperature ΔTy and thus decrease the residual extrusion for the same thermal load. ... Using the average extrusion measured for SG and LG vias, it was found that the corresponding thermal load is around 350°C for both TSVs (red and blue solid lines). ... The effect of grain structures on via extrusion was examined recently by experiments and modeling. ... Nanoindentation measurements were carried out to measure the elastic and plastic properties of the Cu vias. ... The lower yield strength for LG TSVs is consistent with the Hall–Petch relation. ... Interfacial sliding and Cu plasticity have been suggested as two possible mechanisms for via extrusion. ... On the effect of interfacial properties, assuming a perfectly bonded interface between the Cu via and Si, the residual extrusion at room temperature was significantly reduced, by ∼ 3x. ... On the effect of via dimensions, the results show a general trend of decreasing extrusion with smaller via diameter and depth.

gold_context_sources.section_title: Spin-transfer Torque MRAM – Status and Outlook
gold_context_sources.evidence: STT-MRAM has been in production for standalone memory since 2018 and eFlash-replacement applications since 2019. Materials and device innovations are needed to further improve the switching efficiency and lower the switching current. Significant progress has been made by minimizing the current penalty at shorter write pulses and eliminating write-error-rate anomalies at deep error floors. Reliable switching down to 1e-6 and 1e-11 error floors was demonstrated in devices with optimized materials design.

ground_truth_answer: Grain size and interfacial properties significantly influence via extrusion in 3D integration TSVs, impacting their reliability and behavior under thermal cycling. Experimental and modeling studies show that smaller grain sizes (SG TSVs) correlate with lower average via extrusion (117 nm) compared to larger grain sizes (LG TSVs, 147 nm). This is consistent with the Hall–Petch relation where smaller grains increase yield strength, raising the critical temperature for plastic deformation and reducing residual extrusion after thermal cycles. Nanoindentation measurements confirm higher yield strength in SG TSVs, which mitigates plastic deformation during thermal stress. Interfacial properties also play a crucial role; a perfectly bonded Cu via-Si interface can reduce residual extrusion by approximately three times compared to interfaces allowing interfacial sliding. Interfacial sliding leads to increased via extrusion at elevated temperatures, while plastic deformation of Cu causes permanent extrusion even after cooling. The residual extrusion depends on the maximum process temperature and the yield strength of the Cu via, with typical thermal loads around 350°C inducing plastic deformation. Via dimensions further influence extrusion, with smaller diameter and depth generally reducing extrusion; however, reliability concerns remain due to statistical distributions of maximum extrusion (the weakest link effect). Therefore, optimizing grain structure to achieve uniform small grains and improving interfacial adhesion are key strategies to enhance TSV reliability by minimizing via extrusion and its associated mechanical stresses during thermal cycling. These improvements help maintain structural integrity and performance of 3D integrated circuits under typical manufacturing and operational conditions.
